title: Cloudflare incident
description: A bad config (router rule) caused all of their edge routers to crash, taking down all of Cloudflare.

resolution: The erroneous router rule was identified and removed to restore normal operations.
summary: CloudFlare experienced a critical outage affecting all services due to a bad router configuration that caused all edge routers to fail. The issue was resolved by identifying and removing the erroneous rule.

title: Cloudflare incident
description: During a maintenance of their private backbone network, an engineer made a typo in the Atlanta datacenter network configuration, causing all traffic coming from America and Europe flowing to this only datacenter, crushing it.

resolution: The Atlanta router was disabled, and traffic began flowing normally again.
summary: A configuration error in the Atlanta datacenter caused a significant traffic drop and localized outages across the network. The issue was resolved by disabling the Atlanta router, restoring normal traffic flow within 27 minutes.

title: Cloudflare incident
description: A change to our Tiered Cache system caused some requests to fail for users with status code 530. The impact lasted for almost six hours in total. We estimate that about 5% of all requests failed at peak. Because of the complexity of our system and a blind spot in our tests, we did not spot this when the change was released to our test environment.
symptoms: Users experienced requests failing with HTTP Status Code 530, leading to customer reports and alerts triggered by a sharp increase in 530s.
root_cause: A bug in a software release of a CDN component that affected users with Tiered Cache, Cloudflare Images, or Bandwidth Alliance configured.
resolution: The team identified the bug and expedited a rollback, which completed in 87 minutes.
summary: A software release caused a bug that led to HTTP Status Code 530 errors for users, prompting customer complaints. The issue was traced to the Tiered Cache system, and a rollback was executed to resolve it.

title: Cloudflare incident
description: On 4 October 2023, Cloudflare experienced DNS resolution problems starting at 07:00 UTC and ending at 11:00 UTC. Some users of 1.1.1.1 or products like WARP, Zero Trust, or third party DNS resolvers which use 1.1.1.1 may have received SERVFAIL DNS responses to valid queries. We’re very sorry for this outage. This outage was an internal software error and not the result of an attack. In this blog, we’re going to talk about what the failure was, why it occurred, and what we’re doing to make sure this doesn’t happen again.
symptoms: Users of 1.1.1.1 and related products received SERVFAIL DNS responses to valid queries.
root_cause: The 1.1.1.1 resolver systems had problems parsing a new resource record type (ZONEMD) included in the root zones.
resolution: Not specified
summary: On October 4, 2023, Cloudflare experienced DNS resolution issues due to the 1.1.1.1 resolver systems failing to parse a new ZONEMD record. This resulted in SERVFAIL responses for users, and the incident lasted for 4 hours.

title: Cloudflare incident
description: On 18 Nov. 2025, a change in permissions in a database in Cloudflare's bot-detection systems caused a file to be output that exceeded the limits of the software that runs that system. That file was propagated througout Cloudflare's network, causing a systemwide outage.

root_cause: A change in permissions in a database caused the output of a feature file that exceeded the software's size limits.
resolution: Stopped the propagation of the larger-than-expected feature file and replaced it with an earlier version.
summary: On November 18, 2025, Cloudflare experienced a critical outage due to a database permission change that led to an oversized feature file, causing network traffic delivery failures. title: Firefox incident
description: On January 13th, 2022, a specific code path in Firefox's network stack triggered a problem in the HTTP/3 protocol implementation. This blocked network communication and made Firefox unresponsive, unable to load web content for nearly two hours.
symptoms: Firefox became unusable for close to two hours, with a spike in crashes reported and user complaints about the browser hanging.
root_cause: A change by Google Cloud Platform made HTTP/3 the default protocol, which triggered a bug in Firefox's network stack.
resolution: HTTP/3 was explicitly disabled on GCP at 09:12 UTC.
summary: On January 13, 2022, Firefox users experienced a nearly two-hour outage due to a bug in the network stack triggered by an unannounced change in HTTP/3 protocol settings by Google Cloud Platform. The issue was resolved by disabling HTTP/3 on GCP.

title: NPM incident
description: Fastly configuration change caused backend routing issue. To be exact, the issue is that we were setting the req.backend in a vcl_fetch function, and then calling restart to re-parse the rules. However, calling restart will reset the req.backend to the first backed in the list, which in this case happened to be Manta, rather than the load balanced CouchDB servers.
symptoms: All requests were directed to Manta, resulting in 403 Forbidden responses due to Manta's inability to handle certain requests. This led to a thundering herd effect as Fastly did not cache error codes.
root_cause: A bug in the Fastly VCL configuration change that reset req.backend to Manta instead of the intended CouchDB servers.
resolution: Identified the root cause with Fastly support, ensured req.backend is explicitly set in the vcl_recv function, and set up a separate Varnish instance for staging changes.
summary: The incident involved all requests being routed to Manta, causing 403 errors due to a configuration bug in Fastly. The root cause was identified, and steps were taken to ensure proper backend routing in future configurations.

title: OWASA incident
description: The wrong push of a button lead to a water treatment plant shutting down due to too high levels of fluoride.
symptoms: Customers were unable to use or drink their water for more than twenty-four hours; a fluoride level of 5.90 mg/L was recorded, exceeding state and federal standards.
root_cause: An unintentional operator keystroke caused the fluoride feed pump to increase its feed rate to 80 percent instead of the normal 8-12 percent.
resolution: The operator attempted to change the command twelve seconds later, but the change did not register until after 2:40 p.m., when the issue was finally resolved.
summary: A human error led to a fluoride overfeed at OWASA's water treatment plant, causing customers to be unable to use their water for over twenty-four hours. The operator's incorrect command increased the fluoride feed rate significantly, exceeding safety standards. The issue was resolved after the operator attempted to correct the command.

title: Razorpay incident
description: A RDS hardware failure highlighted an incorrect MySQL configuration which resulted in major data loss in a financial system.
symptoms: Engineers observed unusually high numbers of 5xx errors and alerts indicating that the API was unable to connect to its RDS database. Additionally, alerts were received stating that replication on all replicas had failed, displaying a duplicate-key error message.
root_cause: A RDS hardware failure highlighted an incorrect MySQL configuration which resulted in major data loss in a financial system.
resolution: The team shifted traffic from the failed replicas to the master instance and recreated all 5 replicas to restore normal operation.
summary: The incident involved a RDS hardware failure that caused high 5xx errors and replication failures due to an incorrect MySQL configuration. The team resolved the issue by redirecting traffic to the master instance and recreating the replicas.

title: TravisCI incident
description: A configuration issue (incomplete password rotation) led to "leaking" VMs, leading to elevated build queue times.
symptoms: Higher than normal re-queue rates for builds on the OS X platform, leading to a large backlog of OS X builds and errors about being unable to power on new virtual machines.
root_cause: A configuration issue due to incomplete password rotation for the vSphere account used by the vsphere-janitor service, which prevented proper deletion of VMs.
resolution: Paused all OS X builds to stop work on the infrastructure and updated the configuration for the vsphere-janitor service with the new password.
summary: Travis CI experienced high queue times for OS X builds due to a configuration issue where the vsphere-janitor service could not delete VMs because of an incomplete password rotation. The issue was resolved by pausing builds and updating the service configuration.

root_cause: An automated cleanup service deleted stable VM images because they were not included in the internal image catalog due to a hard-coded limit on the number of image names queried.
resolution: Not specified
summary: The incident involved the deletion of stable base VM images, which caused build jobs to stop running. The root cause was a configuration issue in the automated cleanup service that failed to recognize stable images. Resolution steps were not specified.

description: A configuration change made builds start to fail. Manual rollback broke.
symptoms: Builds started to fail after a configuration change; manual rollback broke.
root_cause: A configuration change related to the switch from quay.io to Docker Hub for worker images.
resolution: Not specified
summary: Travis CI experienced build failures due to a configuration change involving the switch to Docker Hub for worker images. The manual rollback attempt was unsuccessful, leading to significant disruption.

title: TravisCI incident
description: Accidental environment variable made tests truncate production database.
symptoms: Users saw blank profiles and were logged in as the wrong users due to database truncation. Alerts were triggered, and there was a backlog of builds.
root_cause: A database query was accidentally run against the production database, truncating all tables.
resolution: The database was restored to its original state, and all affected tokens were revoked.
summary: A major outage occurred at Travis CI when a database query accidentally truncated all tables, leading to user profile issues and incorrect logins. The database was restored, and affected tokens were revoked to resolve the incident.

title: TUI incident
description: Prior to the incident flight the reservation system from which the load sheet was produced had been upgraded. A fault in the system caused female passengers checked in with title ‘Miss’ to be counted as children. The system allocated them a child’s standard weight of 35 kg as opposed to the correct female standard weight of 69 kg. Consequently, with 38 females checked in incorrectly and misidentified as children, the G-TAWG takeoff mass from the load sheet was 1,244 kg below the actual mass of the aircraft.
symptoms: The reservation system incorrectly counted female passengers with the title 'Miss' as children, leading to a significant discrepancy in the aircraft's takeoff mass.
root_cause: A fault in the upgraded reservation system caused misidentification of female passengers as children.
resolution: Not specified
summary: The incident involved a fault in the reservation system that misidentified female passengers as children, resulting in a 1,244 kg discrepancy in the aircraft's takeoff mass. The issue was due to a system upgrade that incorrectly allocated child weight to these passengers.

title: Turso incident
description: Incorrectly configured DB backup identifiers led to data leaks for free tier customers, and the subsequent fix resulted in possible data loss.
symptoms: Data leak and loss in some free tier databases reported on December 4th at 8:10 AM UTC.
root_cause: A bug in the migration process caused some databases to use an empty backup identifier, leading to shared backup storage and subsequent data loss and leaks.
resolution: Re-ran the migration with correct parameters and recreated affected databases with their December 1st backup from the correct backup ID.
summary: On December 4th, a data leak and loss were reported for free tier databases due to a bug that caused some to use an empty backup identifier. The issue was resolved by correcting the migration process and restoring databases from the correct backups.

title: Valve incident
description: Although there's no official postmortem, it looks like a bad BGP config severed Valve's connection to Level 3, Telia, and Abovenet/Zayo, which resulted in a global Steam outage.
symptoms: Users around the world reported being unable to login or use the Steam store, with a dramatic drop in API availability and TCP connection errors.
root_cause: A bad BGP configuration severed Valve's connection to Level 3, Telia, and Abovenet/Zayo.
resolution: Not specified
summary: On May 7th, Steam experienced a critical 2-hour outage affecting millions of users due to a bad BGP configuration that disrupted network connectivity with major ISPs. This resulted in significant TCP connection errors and unavailability of the Steam API.

title: Amazon incident
description: An unknown event caused a transformer to fail. One of the PLCs that checks that generator power is in phase failed for an unknown reason, which prevented a set of backup generators from coming online. This affected EC2, EBS, and RDS in EU West.
symptoms: Customers experienced API errors when targeting requests to the impacted Availability Zone, with launch delays and connectivity issues observed across all EU West Availability Zones.
root_cause: A failure of a 110kV transformer caused a total loss of electricity supply, and a PLC failed to connect backup generators due to a detected large ground fault.
resolution: Further analysis of the PLC and collaboration with the supplier are ongoing to confirm the failure and prevent future occurrences.
summary: A transformer failure led to a loss of power in the EU West Availability Zone, causing significant API errors and connectivity issues. The root cause was linked to a PLC failure that prevented backup generators from activating, and steps are being taken to analyze and resolve the issue.

title: Amazon incident
description: Bad weather caused power failures throughout AWS US East. A single backup generator failed to deliver stable power when power switched over to backup and the generator was loaded. This is despite having passed a load tests two months earlier, and passing weekly power-on tests.
symptoms: Power failures and instability in generator power leading to servers losing power.
root_cause: A single backup generator failed to deliver stable power when switched over, despite passing load tests.
resolution: The backup generator power was stabilized, UPS systems were restarted, and power was restored to the facility.
duration: Approximately 30 minutes from 8:04pm PDT to 8:34pm PDT.
summary: During a severe storm, a backup generator failed to provide stable power, causing servers to lose power. The issue was resolved by stabilizing the generator and restarting the UPS systems.

title: Amazon incident
description: At 10:25pm PDT on June 4, loss of power at an AWS Sydney facility resulting from severe weather in that area lead to disruption to a significant number of instances in an Availability Zone. Due to the signature of the power loss, power  isolation breakers did not engage, resulting in backup energy reserves draining into the degraded power grid.
symptoms: Loss of power to a significant number of EC2 instances and their associated EBS volumes in an Availability Zone, resulting in service disruption.
root_cause: A loss of power at a regional substation due to severe weather caused an unusually long voltage sag, leading to failure of power isolation breakers and subsequent draining of backup energy reserves.
resolution: Power was manually re-engaged by on-site technicians, and automated systems restored power to the affected infrastructure.
duration: From 10:25 PM PDT on June 4 to 1:00 AM PDT on June 5.
summary: The incident involved a significant loss of power to EC2 instances in an Availability Zone due to a voltage sag caused by severe weather, which led to backup systems failing. Power was restored manually and through automated systems, resolving the disruption.

title: ARPANET incident
description: A malfunctioning IMP ([Interface Message Processor](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Interface_Message_Processor)) corrupted routing data, software recomputed checksums propagating bad data with good checksums, incorrect sequence numbers caused buffers to fill, full buffers caused loss of keepalive packets and nodes took themselves off the network. From 1980.
symptoms: Network appeared to be unusable, with no IMP able to communicate reliably, resulting in 'net trouble' error messages and broken connections.
root_cause: A malfunctioning IMP corrupted routing data, leading to faulty network control packets and excessive resource usage by one IMP process.
resolution: Restoring the network to operational condition was a straightforward task with no residual problems once the network was restored.
summary: The ARPANET experienced a high-severity incident where no IMP could communicate reliably due to a malfunctioning IMP that corrupted routing data. The issue was resolved by restoring the network to operational condition without any residual problems.

root_cause: A partial switch misbehavior caused a cascading Byzantine failure.
resolution: Not specified
summary: On November 2, 2020, a partial switch misbehavior led to a cascading Byzantine failure, impacting API and dashboard availability. The incident resulted in a query success rate as low as 75% and significantly slowed dashboard performance for over six hours.

resolution: Most of the control plane was restored at the disaster recovery facility, and services have now been restored for all customers.
duration: From November 2 at 11:44 UTC until November 4 at 04:25 UTC.

title: FirstEnergy / General Electric incident
description: FirstEnergy had a local failure when some transmission lines hit untrimmed foliage. The normal process is to have an alarm go off, which causes human operators to re-distribute power. But the GE system that was monitoring this had a bug which prevented the alarm from getting triggered, which eventually caused a cascading failure that eventually affected 55 million people.
symptoms: Alarm system failure prevented operators from redistributing power after transmission lines dropped in voltage.
root_cause: Software bug in the alarm system at the control room of FirstEnergy.
resolution: Not specified
summary: The Northeast blackout of 2003 was caused by a software bug in FirstEnergy's alarm system, which failed to alert operators to redistribute power, leading to a cascading failure that affected 55 million people. The incident lasted between 2 hours and 4 days, with full restoration taking several days.

title: GitHub incident
description: On January 28th, 2016 GitHub experienced a disruption in the power at their primary datacenter.
symptoms: GitHub was unavailable for two hours and six minutes, with HTTP 503 response codes being delivered and alerts generated to multiple on-call engineers.
root_cause: A disruption in the power at the primary data center caused over 25% of servers and several network devices to reboot, leaving the infrastructure in a partially operational state.
resolution: The response team inspected the infrastructure, correlated findings with initial outage alerts, and implemented DDoS defenses while investigating network health.
summary: On January 28th, 2016, GitHub experienced a high-severity incident due to a power disruption at their primary data center, leading to HTTP 503 errors and service unavailability for over two hours. The response involved inspecting infrastructure and implementing DDoS defenses.

title: Google incident
description: Successive lightning strikes on their European datacenter (europe-west1-b) caused loss of power to Google Compute Engine storage systems within that region. I/O errors were observed on a subset of Standard Persistent Disks (HDDs) and permanent data loss was observed on a small fraction of those.
symptoms: I/O errors were observed on a subset of Standard Persistent Disks, and permanent data loss was observed on a small fraction of those disks.
root_cause: Four successive lightning strikes on the local utilities grid caused loss of power to Google Compute Engine storage systems.
resolution: Google engineers carried out data recovery operations to restore functionality to the affected disks.
summary: The incident involved I/O errors and permanent data loss on Standard Persistent Disks due to lightning strikes affecting power. Recovery operations were conducted to restore functionality to the affected disks.

resolution: Corrected traffic routing on 19 July 2022 at 12:35 US/Pacific.

title: PythonAnywhere incident
description: A storage volume failure on one of storage servers caused a number of outages, starting with PythonAnywhere site and also with our users’ programs (including websites) that were dependent on that volume, and later spreading to other hosted sites.
symptoms: PythonAnywhere site was unavailable or generating excessive errors; users' websites and scheduled tasks were also affected.
root_cause: A storage volume failure on one of the storage servers.
resolution: Not specified
summary: A storage volume failure on a server caused significant outages for PythonAnywhere and its users, leading to unavailability of websites and scheduled tasks. The incident lasted several hours, with the site being down for over two hours.

We have some plans on what our next steps should be, and will be implementing at least some of them over the coming months.

title: Sun incident
description: Sun famously didn't include ECC in a couple generations of server parts. This resulted in data corruption and crashing. Following Sun's typical MO, they made customers that reported a bug sign an NDA before explaining the issue.
symptoms: High-end servers made by Sun Microsystems suddenly crashed, affecting major corporate accounts like America Online and Ebay.
root_cause: An odd problem involving stray cosmic rays and memory chips in the flagship Enterprise server line.
resolution: Sun will make repairs on a one-to-one basis with customers who have had a problem, but will not fix all sold servers.
summary: Sun Microsystems' high-end servers experienced crashes due to cosmic rays affecting memory chips, leading to significant issues for major clients. The company is addressing the problem on a case-by-case basis without a full recall.

title: CCP Games incident
description: A typo and a name conflict caused the installer to sometimes delete the *boot.ini* file on installation of an expansion for *EVE Online* - with [consequences.](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=msXRFJ2ar_E)
symptoms: Players reported that the installer was deleting the C:\boot.ini file, causing some computers to fail to start until the file was fixed.
root_cause: A typo in the installer script led to the deletion of the boot.ini file from the root of the current drive instead of the intended directory.
resolution: The installer script was corrected to ensure that the full path was specified to prevent the deletion of critical system files.
summary: The EVE Online expansion installer caused critical issues by deleting the C:\boot.ini file, leading to startup failures for some players. The root cause was a typo in the installer script, which was subsequently fixed by specifying the full path in the deletion command.

title: GitHub incident
description: A 43 second network partition during maintenance caused MySQL master failover, but the new master didn't have several seconds of writes propogated to it because of cross-continent latency.  24+ hours of restoration work to maintain data integrity.
symptoms: Degraded service for 24 hours and 11 minutes, displaying out of date and inconsistent information, inability to serve webhook events or build and publish GitHub Pages sites.
root_cause: A 43 second network partition during maintenance caused MySQL master failover, with the new master not having several seconds of writes propagated due to cross-continent latency.
resolution: Manual reconciliation for a few seconds of database writes is still in progress.
summary: GitHub experienced degraded service for over 24 hours due to a network partition during maintenance that triggered a MySQL master failover, resulting in inconsistent data. The issue is being addressed through manual reconciliation of database writes.

title: GoCardless incident
description: All queries on a critical PostgreSQL table were blocked by the combination of an extremely fast database migration and a long-running read query, causing 15 seconds of downtime.
symptoms: API requests timing out, alerts triggered during the incident.
root_cause: All queries on a critical PostgreSQL table were blocked due to a combination of an extremely fast database migration and a long-running read query.
resolution: The migration was completed, and the API came back up after 15 seconds.
summary: During a planned database migration, API requests timed out for 15 seconds due to blocking on a critical PostgreSQL table caused by a fast migration and a long-running read query. The issue was resolved once the migration completed.

title: Google incident
description: Many changes to a rarely modified load balancer were applied through a very slow code path. This froze all public addressing changes for ~2 hours.
symptoms: Newly created Google Compute Engine instances, Cloud VPNs, and network load balancers were unavailable via their public IP addresses, preventing outbound traffic and causing health checks to fail.
root_cause: A large set of updates applied to a rarely used load balancing configuration exposed an inefficient code path, causing the canary deployment to time out and queue all public addressing changes.
resolution: Google engineers restarted the jobs responsible for programming changes to the network load balancers, processing the problematic changes in a batch to avoid the inefficient code path.
summary: Newly created Google Compute Engine instances and network resources were unavailable due to an inefficient code path triggered by a large set of updates. The issue was resolved by restarting the programming jobs, allowing normal traffic to resume.

root_cause: A combination of conflicting deployed versions and re-using a previously used bit caused a $460M loss.

title: WebKit code repository incident
description: The WebKit repository, a Subversion repository configured to use deduplication, became unavailable after two files with the same SHA-1 hash were checked in as test data, with the intention of implementing a safety check for collisions. The two files had different md5 sums and so a checkout would fail a consistency check. For context, the first public SHA-1 hash collision had very recently been announced, with an example of two colliding files.
symptoms: The WebKit repository became unavailable, and checkouts failed a consistency check due to two files with the same SHA-1 hash being committed.
root_cause: Two files with differing content but the same SHA-1 hash were committed, causing a failure in the consistency check due to representation sharing in Subversion.
resolution: Not specified
summary: The WebKit repository experienced unavailability and checkout failures after two files with the same SHA-1 hash were committed. The root cause was a collision in the SHA-1 hash due to representation sharing in Subversion.

title: Azure incident
description: Certificates that were valid for one year were created. Instead of using an appropriate library, someone wrote code that computed one year to be the current date plus one year. On February 29th 2012, this resulted in the creation of certificates with an expiration date of February 29th 2013, which were rejected because of the invalid date. This caused an Azure global outage that lasted for most of a day.
symptoms: Certificates with invalid expiration dates were created, leading to service disruptions and user complaints.
root_cause: A software bug caused certificates valid for one year to have an expiration date of February 29th, 2013, which was invalid.
resolution: The initial software bug was detected, responded to, and fixed, followed by addressing the impacted clusters.
summary: On February 29th, 2012, Azure experienced a critical outage due to a software bug that created certificates with invalid expiration dates, leading to service disruptions. The issue was resolved by detecting and fixing the bug, along with addressing the affected clusters.

title: Cloudflare incident
description: Backwards time flow from tracking [the 27th leap second on 2016-12-31T23:59:60Z](https://hpiers.obspm.fr/iers/bul/bulc/bulletinc.52) caused the weighted round-robin selection of DNS resolvers (RRDNS) to panic and fail on some CNAME lookups.  Go's `time.Now()` was incorrectly assumed to be monotonic; this injected negative values into calls to `rand.Int63n()`, which panics in that case.
symptoms: Some DNS resolutions to Cloudflare managed web properties failed, affecting approximately 0.2% of DNS queries and less than 1% of all HTTP requests.
root_cause: The belief that time cannot go backwards led to a negative value being recorded during the leap second, causing the weighted round-robin selection of DNS resolvers to panic.
resolution: The most affected machines were patched in 90 minutes and the fix was rolled out worldwide by 0645 UTC.
summary: Cloudflare experienced DNS resolution failures due to a panic in their RRDNS software caused by a negative value resulting from the leap second. The issue was quickly identified and patched within 90 minutes.

title: Linux incident
description: When a leap second occurred, `CLOCK_REALTIME` was rewound by one second. This was not done via a mechanism that would update `hrtimer base.offset`. This meant that when a timer interrupt happened, TIMER_ABSTIME CLOCK_REALTIME timers got expired one second early, including timers set for less than one second. This caused applications that used sleep for less than one second in a loop to spinwait without sleeping, causing high load on many systems. This caused a large number of web services to go down in 2012.
symptoms: High load on many systems and a large number of web services went down due to applications spinning without sleeping.
root_cause: When a leap second occurred, CLOCK_REALTIME was rewound by one second without updating hrtimer base.offset, causing TIMER_ABSTIME CLOCK_REALTIME timers to expire one second early.
resolution: The fix involved calling clock_was_set when applying a leap second to ensure proper timer behavior.
summary: The incident was caused by a leap second that rewound CLOCK_REALTIME without updating hrtimer base.offset, leading to premature timer expirations and high system load. The issue was resolved by implementing a fix to call clock_was_set during leap second adjustments.

title: Mozilla incident
description: Most Firefox add-ons stopped working around May 4th 2019 when a certificate expired. Firefox requires a valid certificate chain to prevent malware. About nine hours later, Mozilla pushed a privileged add-on that injected a valid certificate into Firefox's certificate store, creating a valid chain and unblocking add-ons. This disabled effectively all add-ons, about 15,000, and the resolution took approximately 15-21 hours for most users. Some user data was lost. Previously Mozilla [posted](https://web.archive.org/web/20250408175304/https://hacks.mozilla.org/2019/05/technical-details-on-the-recent-firefox-add-on-outage/) about the technical details.
symptoms: Most Firefox add-ons stopped working, affecting about 15,000 add-ons, and some user data was lost.
root_cause: The certificate required for Firefox add-ons expired due to a misunderstanding by the responsible team regarding expiration dates and a lack of testing for certificate expiration in the QA plan.
resolution: Mozilla pushed a privileged add-on that injected a valid certificate into Firefox's certificate store, creating a valid chain and unblocking add-ons.
summary: The incident involved a critical outage of Firefox add-ons due to an expired certificate, which was resolved by injecting a valid certificate into the browser's store, restoring functionality for users.

title: Github incident
description: Github platform encountered a novel failure mode when processing a schema migration on a large MySQL table. Schema migrations are a common task at GitHub and often take weeks to complete. The final step in a migration is to perform a rename to move the updated table into the correct place. During the final step of this migration a significant portion of our MySQL read replicas entered a semaphore deadlock. Our MySQL clusters consist of a primary node for write traffic, multiple read replicas for production traffic, and several replicas that serve internal read traffic for backup and analytics purposes. The read replicas that hit the deadlock entered a crash-recovery state causing an increased load on healthy read replicas. Due to the cascading nature of this scenario, there were not enough active read replicas to handle production requests which impacted the availability of core GitHub services.
symptoms: Significant impact and degraded state of availability for core GitHub services, including GitHub Actions, API Requests, Codespaces, Git Operations, Issues, GitHub Packages, GitHub Pages, Pull Requests, and Webhooks.
root_cause: A significant portion of MySQL read replicas entered a semaphore deadlock during the final step of a schema migration.
resolution: Promoted all available internal replicas into the production path, prioritized data integrity by removing production traffic from broken replicas, and restored capacity once replicas recovered.
summary: GitHub experienced a critical incident where MySQL read replicas entered a semaphore deadlock during a schema migration, impacting core services. The issue was resolved by promoting healthy replicas and prioritizing data integrity, allowing for a return to normal operations.

title: Amazon incident
description: Human error. On February 28th 2017 9:37AM PST, the Amazon S3 team was debugging a minor issue. Despite using an established playbook, one of the commands intending to remove a small number of servers was issued with a typo, inadvertently causing a larger set of servers to be removed. These servers supported critical S3 systems. As a result, dependent systems required a full restart to correctly operate, and the system underwent widespread outages for US-EAST-1 (Northern Virginia) until final resolution at 1:54PM PST. Since Amazon's own services such as EC2 and EBS rely on S3 as well, it caused a vast cascading failure which affected hundreds of companies.
symptoms: Widespread outages in US-EAST-1, inability to service S3 requests, and cascading failures affecting dependent services like EC2 and EBS.
root_cause: Human error due to a typo in a command that removed a larger set of servers than intended.
resolution: Restarted the affected S3 subsystems, including the index and placement subsystems, to restore functionality.
duration: From 9:37AM PST to 1:54PM PST, approximately 4 hours and 17 minutes.
summary: The incident involved widespread outages in US-EAST-1 due to a human error that removed critical servers, leading to a failure in servicing S3 requests. The issue was resolved by restarting the affected subsystems, restoring functionality after several hours.

title: Amazon incident
description: Message corruption caused the distributed server state function to overwhelm resources on the S3 request processing fleet.
symptoms: Error rates in all Amazon S3 datacenters began to quickly climb, with very few requests completing successfully.
root_cause: Message corruption caused the server-to-server communication problems.
resolution: Shut down all communication between Amazon S3 servers, shut down all components used for request processing, cleared the system's state, and reactivated the request processing components.
duration: From 8:40am PDT to 4:58pm PDT, approximately 8 hours and 18 minutes.
summary: Amazon S3 experienced a critical incident where error rates surged due to message corruption affecting server communication. The issue was resolved by shutting down and clearing the system state, followed by reactivating request processing components.

title: Amazon incident
description: Human error during a routine networking upgrade led to a resource crunch, exacerbated by software bugs, that ultimately resulted in an outage across all US East Availability Zones as well as a loss of 0.07% of volumes.

root_cause: Human error during a routine networking upgrade led to a resource crunch, exacerbated by software bugs.
resolution: Disabled all control APIs for EBS in the affected Availability Zone to restore volumes and stabilize the EBS cluster.
summary: The incident involved EBS volumes becoming unresponsive due to human error during a networking upgrade, leading to widespread service disruption. The resolution involved disabling control APIs to restore functionality.

title: Amazon incident
description: Inability to contact a data collection server triggered a latent memory leak bug in the reporting agent on the storage servers. And there is no graceful degradation handling, thus the reporting agent continuously contacted the collection server in a way that slowly consumed system memory. Also the monitoring system failed to alarm this EBS server's memory leak, also EBS servers generally make very dynamic use of all memory. By Monday morning, the rate of memory loss became quite high and confused enough memory on the affected storage servers which cannot keep with the request handling process. This error got further severed by the inability to do the failover, which resulted in the outage.
symptoms: Degraded performance of EBS volumes, inability to process I/O requests, increased number of stuck volumes, and failure to alarm on memory leak.
root_cause: A latent memory leak bug in the reporting agent triggered by the inability to contact a data collection server due to a DNS update failure.
resolution: Not specified
summary: The incident involved degraded performance and stuck EBS volumes due to a memory leak in the reporting agent, caused by a DNS update failure preventing contact with a data collection server.

description: Elastic Load Balancer ran into problems when "a maintenance process that was inadvertently run against the production ELB state data".

root_cause: A maintenance process was inadvertently run against the production ELB state data, resulting in the logical deletion of a portion of this data.
resolution: The ELB technical team disabled several of the ELB control plane workflows to prevent additional service impact and focused on recovering the missing ELB state data.
summary: The Amazon ELB service experienced high latency and errors due to the inadvertent deletion of critical state data during a maintenance process, leading to performance issues for customer applications. The technical team disabled workflows to mitigate further impact and worked on recovering the missing data.

root_cause: A brief network disruption caused metadata service responses to exceed the allowed retrieval and transmission time.
resolution: Not specified
summary: A network disruption led to metadata service response times exceeding timeout values, causing storage nodes to disqualify themselves from accepting requests. The incident's resolution steps were not specified.

title: Amazon incident
description: Scaling the front-end cache fleet for Kinesis caused all of the servers in the fleet to exceed the maximum number of threads allowed by an operating system configuration. Multiple critical downstream services affected, from Cognito to Lambda to CloudWatch.
symptoms: Errors on putting and getting Kinesis records, alarms firing, and a variety of errors observed in calls made by front-end fleet members.
root_cause: Scaling the front-end cache fleet for Kinesis caused all servers in the fleet to exceed the maximum number of threads allowed by an operating system configuration.
resolution: The new capacity was removed while researching other errors, and a full restart of the front-end fleet was determined to be necessary.
summary: The incident involved errors in Kinesis record operations due to exceeding thread limits in the front-end cache fleet, leading to a critical service disruption. The resolution involved removing the new capacity and planning a full fleet restart.

title: Amazon incident
description: At 7:30 AM PST, an automated activity to scale capacity of one of the AWS services hosted in the main AWS network triggered an unexpected behavior from a large number of clients inside the internal network. This resulted in a large surge of connection activity that overwhelmed the networking devices between the internal network and the main AWS network, resulting in delays for communication between these networks. These delays increased latency and errors for services communicating between these networks, resulting in even more connection attempts and retries. This led to persistent congestion and performance issues on the devices connecting the two networks.
symptoms: Increased latency and errors for services communicating between the internal network and the main AWS network, elevated internal DNS errors, and persistent congestion impacting real-time monitoring data.
root_cause: An automated activity to scale capacity of one of the AWS services triggered unexpected behavior from a large number of clients, overwhelming the networking devices between the internal network and the main AWS network.
resolution: The team moved the internal DNS traffic away from the congested network paths, which improved the availability of several impacted services.
summary: The incident involved increased latency and errors due to an automated scaling activity that overwhelmed networking devices, leading to congestion. The resolution involved rerouting internal DNS traffic, which improved service availability but did not fully resolve the congestion.

title: AppNexus incident
description: A double free revealed by a database update caused all "impression bus" servers to crash simultaneously. This wasn't caught in staging and made it into production because a time delay is required to trigger the bug, and the staging period didn't have a built-in delay.
symptoms: The AppNexus platform experienced a technical failure that initially fully halted ad serving and later partially degraded ad serving.
root_cause: A double free revealed by a database update caused all 'impression bus' servers to crash simultaneously.
resolution: Engineers restored service in AMS1 using a new snapshot of data from the internal database that was tested and shown to not trigger the bug.
duration: Approximately two and a half hours.
summary: The AppNexus platform faced a critical outage due to a double free from a database update, halting ad serving. Engineers quickly restored service using a tested snapshot of data, resolving the issue.

description: , [see also](https://web.archive.org/web/20220530044506/https://m.signalvnoise.com/update-on-basecamp-3-being-stuck-in-read-only-as-of-nov-8-922am-cst/). In November 2018 a database hit the integer limit, leaving the service in read-only mode.

root_cause: The database hit the integer limit of 2,147,483,647 on the events table, which tracks almost every activity in Basecamp.
resolution: The capacity of the events table was expanded by updating the database schema to accommodate a higher limit.
summary: Basecamp experienced a critical outage where users were unable to modify or add information due to the database hitting its integer limit. The issue was resolved by expanding the events table's capacity, which had been a known problem that was not addressed in time.

title: BBC Online incident
description: In July 2014, BBC Online experienced a very long outage of several of its popular online services including the BBC iPlayer. When the database backend was overloaded, it had started to throttle requests from various services. Services that hadn't cached the database responses locally began timing out and eventually failed completely.
symptoms: Requests for metadata to the application servers started to fail, making BBC iPlayer and other services inaccessible. The emergency homepage was displayed due to technical problems.
root_cause: The database backend was overloaded, causing throttling of requests, and a failure in the caching layer that affected multiple products.
resolution: Restored the caching layer and isolated the source of the additional load on the database, eventually stabilizing the cache.
summary: BBC Online experienced a critical outage when the database backend was overloaded, leading to failed requests for metadata and inaccessible services like BBC iPlayer. The incident was resolved by restoring the caching layer and stabilizing the database load.

title: BrowserStack incident
description: An old prototype machine with the [Shellshock](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Shellshock_(software_bug)) vulnerability still active had secret keys on it which ultimately led to a security breach of the Production system.
symptoms: Alerts were raised on the monitoring system due to a database table being locked during a copy operation, and an unrecognized IP was detected in the logs.
root_cause: An old prototype machine with the Shellshock vulnerability was exploited, allowing unauthorized access to AWS API keys and subsequent data retrieval.
resolution: The service was temporarily taken down to scrutinize each component, and the unrecognized IP was blocked immediately upon detection.
summary: BrowserStack experienced a security breach due to an old prototype machine vulnerable to Shellshock, which allowed unauthorized access to user data. The incident was resolved by taking down the service and blocking the attacker's IP.

title: CircleCI incident
description: A GitHub outage and recovery caused an unexpectedly large incoming load. For reasons that aren't specified, a large load causes CircleCI's queue system to slow down, in this case to handling one transaction per minute.
symptoms: Queue length was rising, but build capacity was not fully utilized; dequeuing on the order of one build per minute; long list of queued builds; queries taking a massive amount of time to run; many queries timing out.
root_cause: A severe degradation in database performance caused by a sustained surge in build requests from GitHub, leading to significant resource contention across everything accessing the same database.
resolution: Initially attempted to salvage the queue, but ultimately decided to delete the queue as the value of processing the builds dropped significantly.
summary: CircleCI experienced a critical incident where a surge in build requests from GitHub led to severe database performance degradation, causing the build queue to lock up. The resolution involved deleting the queue after attempts to salvage it were deemed ineffective.

title: CircleCI incident
description: By January 4, 2023, our internal investigation had determined the scope of the intrusion by the unauthorized third party and the entry path of the attack. To date, we have learned that an unauthorized third party leveraged malware deployed to a CircleCI engineer’s laptop in order to steal a valid, 2FA-backed SSO session. This machine was compromised on December 16, 2022. The malware was not detected by our antivirus software. Our investigation indicates that the malware was able to execute session cookie theft, enabling them to impersonate the targeted employee in a remote location and then escalate access to a subset of our production systems.
symptoms: Suspicious GitHub OAuth activity reported by a customer, leading to a deeper review by CircleCI’s security team.
root_cause: Malware deployed to a CircleCI engineer’s laptop stole a valid, 2FA-backed SSO session.

description: A parser bug caused Cloudflare edge servers to return memory that contained private information such as HTTP cookies, authentication tokens, HTTP POST bodies, and other sensitive data.
symptoms: Corrupted web pages being returned by HTTP requests through Cloudflare.
root_cause: A parser bug caused edge servers to return memory past the end of a buffer, leaking private information.
resolution: Turned off three minor Cloudflare features using the same HTML parser chain, preventing memory from being returned in HTTP responses.
duration: The greatest period of impact was from February 13 to February 18, with a fix implemented globally in under 7 hours.
summary: Cloudflare experienced corrupted web pages due to a parser bug that leaked private information. The issue was resolved by disabling affected features, with a global fix implemented in under 7 hours.

title: Cloudflare incident

root_cause: A poorly written regular expression in a WAF rule caused excessive backtracking, leading to CPU exhaustion.
resolution: The problematic WAF rule was identified and removed to restore normal service.
summary: A critical incident at Cloudflare on July 2, 2019, resulted in 27 minutes of downtime due to a poorly written WAF rule that caused CPU exhaustion, leading to widespread 502 errors. The issue was resolved by removing the problematic rule.

title: Discord incident
description: "At approximately 14:01, a Redis instance acting as the primary for a highly-available cluster used by Discord's API services was migrated automatically by Google’s Cloud Platform. This migration caused the node to incorrectly drop offline, forcing the cluster to rebalance and trigger known issues with the way Discord API instances handle Redis failover. After resolving this partial outage, unnoticed issues on other services caused a cascading failure through Discord’s real time system. These issues caused enough critical impact that Discord’s engineering team was forced to fully restart the service, reconnecting millions of clients over a period of 20 minutes."
symptoms: Engineers were alerted to anomalies via real time metrics and monitoring, observed abnormal latency on API endpoints, and received end user reports of servers appearing offline.
root_cause: A Redis instance was automatically migrated by Google’s Cloud Platform, causing it to drop offline and trigger known issues with Redis failover handling in API instances.
resolution: Engineers executed a rolling restart of all API instances, corrected a misconfigured edge caching rule, disabled the problematic API endpoint, and recovered a non-critical Cassandra cluster.
summary: The incident was triggered by an automatic migration of a Redis instance, leading to failover issues and cascading failures in Discord's real time system. Engineers resolved the situation by restarting API instances and correcting configuration errors, ultimately restoring service.

title: European Space Agency incident
description: An overflow occurred when converting a 16-bit number to a 64-bit numer in the Ariane 5 intertial guidance system, causing the rocket to crash. The actual overflow occurred in code that wasn't necessary for operation but was running anyway. According to [one account](https://web.archive.org/web/20120829114850/https://www.around.com/ariane.html), this caused a diagnostic error message to get printed out, and the diagnostic error message was somehow interpreted as actual valid data. According to [another account](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cluster_%28spacecraft%29?oldid=217305667), no trap handler was installed for the overflow.
symptoms: The rocket veered off its flight path and was destroyed by its automated destruct system, resulting in the loss of the payload and significant financial loss.
root_cause: An arithmetic overflow occurred when converting a 64-bit floating point number to a 16-bit signed integer, compounded by the disabling of the software handler for this error trap.
resolution: Not specified
summary: The Ariane 5 rocket experienced a critical failure due to an arithmetic overflow in the control software, leading to its destruction shortly after launch. The incident highlighted significant design and management issues, as pre-flight tests did not account for the new flight conditions.

title: Foursquare incident
description: MongoDB fell over under load when it ran out of memory. The failure was catastrophic and not graceful due to a a query pattern that involved a read-load with low levels of locality (each user check-in caused a read of all check-ins for the user's history, and records were 300 bytes with no spatial locality, meaning that most of the data pulled in from each page was unnecessary). A lack of monitoring on the MongoDB instances caused the high load to go undetected until the load became catastrophic, causing 17 hours of downtime spanning two incidents in two days.
symptoms: The system experienced a catastrophic failure with 17 hours of downtime, and initial outage reports were blaming MongoDB or stating the problem was unknown.
root_cause: MongoDB fell over under load when it ran out of memory due to a query pattern with low levels of locality and a lack of monitoring on the MongoDB instances.
resolution: Not specified
summary: Foursquare experienced a critical outage lasting 17 hours due to MongoDB running out of memory from a poor query pattern and lack of monitoring, leading to a catastrophic failure.

title: Google incident
description: Filestore enforces a global limit on API requests to limit impact in overload scenarios. The outage was triggered when an internal Google service managing a large number of GCP projects malfunctioned and overloaded the Filestore API with requests, causing global throttling of the Filestore API. This continued until the internal service was manually paused. As a result of this throttling, read-only API access was unavailable for all customers. This affected customers in all locations, due to a global quota that applies to Filestore. Console, gcloud and API access (List, GetOperation, etc.) calls all failed for a duration of 3 hours, 12 minutes. Mutate operations (CreateInstance, UpdateInstance, CreateBackup, etc.) still succeeded, but customers were unable to check on operation progress.
symptoms: All read-only API requests failed with error code 429 globally, affecting customers in all locations. Console, gcloud, and API access calls failed for a duration of 3 hours, 12 minutes.
root_cause: An internal Google service managing a large number of GCP projects malfunctioned and overloaded the Filestore API with requests, causing global throttling.
resolution: The internal service was manually paused to stop the overload on the Filestore API.
summary: The incident involved a global failure of read-only API requests for Google Cloud Filestore due to an overload caused by an internal service malfunction. The issue was resolved by manually pausing the internal service, restoring API access.

title: Google incident
description: A policy change containing blank fields triggered a null pointer exception in Service Control, Google's API management and control plane system. The code path that failed was not feature flag protected and lacked proper error handling. When the policy data replicated globally, it caused Service Control binaries to crash loop across all regions. While a red-button fix was deployed within 40 minutes, larger regions like us-central-1 experienced extended recovery times (up to 2h 40m) due to a thundering herd problem when Service Control tasks restarted, overloading the underlying Spanner infrastructure. The incident affected Google and Google Cloud APIs globally, with recovery times varying by product architecture.
symptoms: Service Control binaries crashed and looped across all regions, affecting multiple Google and Google Cloud APIs globally.
root_cause: A policy change containing blank fields triggered a null pointer exception in Service Control, which was not feature flag protected and lacked proper error handling.
resolution: A red-button fix was deployed within 40 minutes.
duration: Incident began at 2025-06-12 10:51 and ended at 2025-06-12 18:18, lasting 7 hours and 27 minutes.
summary: A null pointer exception caused by a policy change with blank fields led to Service Control crashes, affecting multiple APIs. A red-button fix was deployed within 40 minutes, but recovery times varied significantly due to a thundering herd problem.

title: Homebrew incident
description: A GitHub personal access token with recently elevated scopes was leaked from Homebrew’s Jenkins that allowed access to `git push` on several Homebrew repositories.
symptoms: A GitHub personal access token was leaked, allowing unauthorized access to git push on Homebrew repositories.
root_cause: A GitHub personal access token with recently elevated scopes was leaked from Homebrew’s Jenkins.
resolution: The leaked credentials were revoked, replaced, and sanitised within Jenkins; branch protection and required reviews were enabled on repositories.
summary: A GitHub personal access token leak from Homebrew’s Jenkins allowed unauthorized git push access. The incident was resolved by revoking and replacing the token, and implementing branch protection and review requirements.
content_preview: Security Incident Disclosure — Homebrew Homebrew Security Incident Disclosure 05 August 2018 MikeMcQuaid On 31st July 2018 a security researcher identified a GitHub personal access token with recently elevated scopes was leaked from Homebrew’s Jenkins that gave them access to git push on Homebrew/brew and Homebrew/homebrew-core. resolution: Optimizations were added and limits raised in the stack to increase capacity for queries; monitoring and manual restarts were performed.
summary: Engineers observed alerts and ingestion delays due to increased load from large customer queries, which were caused by an under-provisioned ingestion pipeline. The resolution involved optimizing the stack and increasing query capacity.

title: Honeycomb incident
description: On September 8th, 2022, our ingest system went down repeatedly and caused interruptions for over eight hours. We will first cover the background behind the incident with a high-level view of the relevant architecture, how we tried to investigate and fix the system, and finally, we’ll go over some meaningful elements that surfaced from our incident review process.
symptoms: Shepherd hosts were running out of memory (OOM) and restarting, leading to cascading crashes in the Refinery cluster. Engineers observed a persistent, metastable failure loop and OOM errors recurred in both Shepherd and Refinery.
root_cause: A failure in the Refinery cluster caused Shepherd to accumulate and queue up data, which led to running out of space and being killed.
resolution: Engineers slowed rollouts to stabilize the system, implemented aggressive sampling to reduce data in the Refinery cluster, and pinned builds to prevent further deployments.
summary: The incident involved persistent OOM errors in Shepherd hosts due to a failure in the Refinery cluster, which caused data accumulation. Engineers stabilized the system by slowing rollouts and implementing aggressive sampling.

title: Honeycomb incident
description: On July 25th, 2023, we experienced a total Honeycomb outage. It impacted all user-facing components from 1:40 p.m. UTC to 2:48 p.m. UTC, during which no data could be processed or accessed. The full details of incident triage process is covered in [here](https://www.honeycomb.io/wp-content/uploads/2023/08/Incident-Review-What-Comes-Up-Must-First-Go-Down.pdf).
symptoms: Engineers observed a marginally slow, inconsistent performance burn in the ingest service, leading to a total outage where no data could be processed or accessed.
root_cause: A subtle implementation bug caused by a feature flag switch that prevented writes from migrating to the correct cluster, compounded by a failure to restart hosts after the flag was toggled.
resolution: A command was written to restart the query engine’s cluster, but the database seized up before it could be executed, leading to a total system crash.
summary: The incident involved a total outage of Honeycomb services due to a subtle implementation bug related to a feature flag switch, which prevented data writes and led to a system crash. Despite attempts to restart the query engine, the database became unresponsive, resulting in a complete service failure.

title: incident.io incident
description: A bad event (poison pill) in the async workers queue triggered unhandled panics that repeatedly crashed the app. This combined poorly with Heroku infrastructure, making it difficult to find the source of the problem. Applied mitigations that are generally interesting to people running web services, such as catching corner cases of Go panic recovery and splitting work by type/class to improve reliability.
symptoms: Intermittent downtime from repeated crashes, indicated by 'H10 - App crashed' error code from Heroku.
root_cause: A bad event (poison pill) in the async workers queue triggered unhandled panics that repeatedly crashed the app.
resolution: Implemented mitigations including handling message panics and separating the app by work type/class.
duration: 13 minutes of downtime over a period of 32 minutes.
summary: The incident involved intermittent downtime due to unhandled panics caused by a poison pill in the async workers queue, leading to repeated crashes. The issue was resolved by implementing panic handling and separating application workloads.

title: Indian Electricity Grid incident
description: One night in July 2012, a skewed electricity supply-demand profile developed when the northern grid drew a tremendous amount of power from the western and eastern grids. Following a series of circuit breakers tripping by virtue of under-frequency protection, the entire NEW (northern-eastern-western) grid collapsed due to the absence of islanding mechanisms. While the grid was reactivated after over 8 hours, similar conditions in the following day caused the grid to fail again. However, the restoration effort concluded almost 24 hours after the occurrence of the latter incident.
symptoms: The northern grid drew a tremendous amount of power from the western and eastern grids, leading to circuit breakers tripping and grid collapse.
root_cause: Absence of islanding mechanisms caused the entire NEW grid to collapse due to under-frequency protection.
resolution: The grid was reactivated after over 8 hours, but similar conditions caused a second failure the following day, requiring almost 24 hours for restoration.
duration: Over 8 hours for the first incident and almost 24 hours for the second incident.
summary: The northern grid experienced a collapse due to excessive power draw and lack of islanding mechanisms, resulting in circuit breakers tripping. The grid was restored after significant downtime, highlighting critical hardware vulnerabilities.

title: Instapaper incident
description: Also [this](https://web.archive.org/web/20240911020547/https://blog.instapaper.com/post/157027537441). Limits were hit for a hosted database. It took many hours to migrate over to a new database.
symptoms: The bookmarks table began to throw errors when attempting to insert new entries after exceeding the 2TB file size limit.
root_cause: The data failure was caused by a 2TB file size limit for RDS instances created before April 2014, specifically affecting the bookmarks table.
resolution: Migrated to a new database to resolve the file size limitation issue.
duration: From February 9 at 12:30PM PT to February 10 at 7:30PM PT.
summary: Instapaper experienced a critical outage when the bookmarks table exceeded the 2TB file size limit, causing errors on new entries. The issue was resolved by migrating to a new database, restoring full service after several hours.

title: Intel incident
description: A scripting bug caused the generation of the divider logic in the Pentium to very occasionally produce incorrect results. The bug wasn't caught in testing because of an incorrect assumption in a proof of correctness. (See [the Wikipedia article on 1994 FDIV bug](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Pentium_FDIV_bug) for more information.)
symptoms: Floating point division operations on the original Intel Pentium CPU produced slightly erroneous results, leading to user complaints about incorrect calculations.
root_cause: A scripting bug caused the generation of the divider logic to occasionally produce incorrect results due to an incorrect assumption in a proof of correctness.
resolution: Not specified
summary: The Intel Pentium FDIV Bug resulted in incorrect floating point division calculations due to a scripting bug in the divider logic, leading to significant user complaints and damage to Intel's public image.

title: Joyent incident
description: An operator used a tool with lax input validation to reboot a small number of servers undergoing maintenance but forgot to type `-n` and instead rebooted all servers in the datacenter. This caused an outage that lasted 2.5 hours, rebooted all customer instances, put tremendous load on DHCP/TFTP PXE boot systems, and left API systems requiring manual intervention. See also [Bryan Cantrill's talk](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=30jNsCVLpAE).
symptoms: All us-east-1 API systems and customer instances were simultaneously rebooted, causing downtime and load on DHCP/TFTP PXE boot systems.
root_cause: An operator used a tool with lax input validation and forgot to type `-n`, resulting in the reboot of all servers in the datacenter.
resolution: The API was restored and service was fully available after manual intervention.
summary: An operator error led to the simultaneous reboot of all us-east-1 API systems and customer instances, causing a 2.5-hour outage. The issue was resolved with manual intervention to restore service.

title: Kickstarter incident
description: Primary DB became inconsistent with all replicas, which wasn't detected until a query failed. This was caused by a MySQL bug which sometimes caused `order by` to be ignored.

root_cause: A MySQL bug which sometimes caused 'order by' to be ignored.
resolution: Engineers stabilized the site, minimized effects of stale replicas, communicated the issue to users, and recovered.
summary: Kickstarter experienced a critical incident where the primary database became inconsistent with all replicas due to a MySQL bug. Engineers stabilized the site and communicated with users while recovering from the issue.

title: Mailgun incident
description: Secondary MongoDB servers became overloaded and while troubleshooting accidentally pushed a change that sent all secondary traffic to the primary MongoDB server, overloading it as well and exacerbating the problem.
symptoms: Engineers observed elevated error rates with API, SMTP, and website services, along with alerts for click/open tracking services and primary MongoDB clusters.
root_cause: Secondary MongoDB servers became overloaded, and a change was inadvertently pushed that redirected all secondary traffic to the primary MongoDB server, overloading it.
resolution: The engineering team deployed connection limits on the primary MongoDB server and re-deployed configuration changes to prefer secondary servers for reads, stabilizing the system.
summary: The incident involved elevated error rates in services due to overloaded MongoDB servers, exacerbated by a misconfiguration that redirected traffic. The issue was resolved by implementing connection limits and reverting to preferred configurations for secondary reads.

resolution: Not specified

title: NASA incident
description: Use of different units of measurement (metric vs. English) caused Mars Climate Orbiter to fail. There were also organizational and procedural failures[[ref](https://space.stackexchange.com/a/20241)] and defects in the navigation software[[ref](https://spectrum.ieee.org/aerospace/robotic-exploration/why-the-mars-probe-went-off-course)].
symptoms: Communication with the spacecraft was permanently lost as it went into orbital insertion.
root_cause: Measurement mismatch between SI units (metric) used by NASA and US customary units used by Lockheed Martin.
resolution: Not specified
summary: The Mars Climate Orbiter failed when communication was lost during orbital insertion due to a measurement mismatch between metric and US customary units. This critical configuration error led to the spacecraft being destroyed in the Martian atmosphere.

title: NASA incident
description: NASA's Mars Pathfinder spacecraft experienced system resets a few days after landing on Mars (1997).  Debugging features were remotely enabled until the cause was found: a [priority inversion](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Priority_inversion) problem in the VxWorks operating system.  The OS software was remotely patched (all the way to Mars) to fix the problem by adding priority inheritance to the task scheduler.
symptoms: System resets were observed a few days after landing on Mars.
root_cause: A priority inversion problem in the VxWorks operating system.
resolution: The OS software was remotely patched by adding priority inheritance to the task scheduler.
summary: NASA's Mars Pathfinder spacecraft experienced system resets due to a priority inversion issue in the VxWorks OS. The problem was resolved by remotely patching the software to implement priority inheritance in the task scheduler.

title: Pagerduty incident
description: In April 2013, [Pagerduty](https://web.archive.org/web/20220906003007/https://www.pagerduty.com/), a cloud service proving application uptime monitoring and real-time notifications, suffered an outage when two of its three independent cloud deployments in different data centers began experiencing connectivity issues and high network latency. It was found later that the two independent deployments shared a common peering point which was experiencing network instability.  While the third deployment was still operational, Pagerduty's applications failed to establish quorum due to to high network latency and hence failed in their ability to send notifications.
symptoms: Delays in notifications and 500s on API endpoints; connectivity issues and high network latency observed.
root_cause: Two independent cloud deployments shared a common peering point which experienced network instability.
resolution: PagerDuty's notification dispatch system was able to reach quorum and started processing queued notifications after the connectivity issue ended.
summary: PagerDuty experienced critical delays in notifications due to high network latency caused by a shared peering point between two cloud deployments. The issue was resolved when the notification dispatch system regained quorum after the connectivity issue was resolved.

resolution: Not specified
summary: Reddit experienced a critical outage lasting over 5 hours on Pi Day, where users encountered error messages and an inaccessible homepage due to a failed Kubernetes cluster upgrade caused by changing node metadata.

title: Roblox incident
description: Roblox end Oct 2021 73 hours outage. Issues with Consul streaming and BoltDB.
symptoms: Extended downtime affecting fifty million daily players, with critical monitoring systems failing to provide visibility into the outage.
root_cause: Enabling a new streaming feature on Consul under high load caused excessive contention and poor performance, compounded by a pathological performance issue in BoltDB.
resolution: Roblox Engineering and HashiCorp teams collaborated to diagnose the issues, improve monitoring, remove circular dependencies, and remediate the root causes in Consul.
summary: Roblox experienced a 73-hour outage due to performance issues in Consul and BoltDB under high load, leading to significant downtime. The resolution involved collaboration between Roblox and HashiCorp to diagnose and remediate the underlying issues.

root_cause: A combination of factors led to a massive disconnection-reconnection process that exceeded database capacity, causing cascading connection failures.
resolution: Not specified
summary: Slack experienced a significant incident where 5% of users were disconnected due to a database capacity issue caused by a massive reconnection process. The incident lasted up to 2 hours, severely impacting user connectivity.

title: Spotify incident
description: Lack of exponential backoff in a microservice caused a cascading failure, leading to notable service degradation.
symptoms: High latency when playing music and some users were unable to log in.
root_cause: Lack of exponential backoff in a microservice caused a cascading failure.
resolution: The Popcount developers deployed a fix to fast fail and return an empty list of subscribers when the service became slow.
summary: A major outage affected European users due to high latency and login issues caused by a lack of exponential backoff in the Popcount microservice. The issue was resolved by implementing a fast fail mechanism to return an empty list of subscribers.

title: Square incident
description: A cascading error from an adjacent service lead to merchant authentication service being overloaded. This impacted merchants for ~2 hours.
symptoms: Engineers observed timeouts from the customer authentication service and reported failures in services depending on merchant identity.
root_cause: Merchant authentication service unrecoverably overloaded due to bug in adjacent service
resolution: Rolled back all software changes leading up to the incident and activated a cross-functional crisis team to follow the disaster response plan.
duration: Service outage for all merchants between 10:02AM-11:55AM; 2FA outage until 1:12PM
summary: A cascading error from an adjacent service caused the merchant authentication service to become overloaded, leading to widespread service outages. The issue was resolved by rolling back recent changes and activating a crisis team.

title: Stackdriver incident
description: In October 2013, [Stackdriver](https://www.stackdriver.com/), experienced an outage, when its Cassandra cluster crashed. Data published by various services into a message bus was being injested into the Cassandra cluster. When the cluster failed, the failure percolated to various producers, that ended up blocking on queue insert operations, eventually leading to the failure of the entire application.
symptoms: The application failed as various producers were blocking on queue insert operations.
root_cause: The Cassandra cluster crashed, leading to a failure in data ingestion.
resolution: Not specified
summary: In October 2013, Stackdriver experienced a critical outage when its Cassandra cluster crashed, causing producers to block on queue insert operations and ultimately leading to the failure of the entire application.

title: Stack Exchange incident
description: Enabling StackEgg for all users resulted in heavy load on load balancers and consequently, a DDoS.
symptoms: Heavy load on load balancers, resulting in a 6 minute public outage on Stack Overflow.
root_cause: Enabling StackEgg for all users led to a significant increase in concurrent sessions due to additional AJAX requests.
resolution: Raised the concurrent sessions limit in HAProxy and reduced the number of HTTP requests from StackEgg by embedding data directly in the page and increasing dependence on websockets.
summary: The incident was caused by enabling StackEgg for all users, which resulted in excessive load on load balancers and a 6 minute outage. The issue was resolved by increasing the concurrent sessions limit and optimizing HTTP requests.

title: Stack Exchange incident
description: Backtracking implementation in the underlying regex engine turned out to be very expensive for a particular post leading to health-check failures and eventual outage.
symptoms: Health-check failures and eventual outage, with the home page not responding fast enough.
root_cause: A malformed post caused a regular expression to consume high CPU on web servers.
resolution: Identified the cause in 10 minutes, wrote the fix in 14 minutes, and rolled it out in 10 minutes.
summary: On July 20, 2016, Stack Exchange experienced a 34-minute outage due to a malformed post that caused a regular expression to consume excessive CPU resources. The issue was identified and resolved by replacing the problematic regex with a substring function.

title: Stack Exchange incident
description: The primary SQL-Server triggered a bugcheck on the SQL Server process, causing the Stack Exchange sites to go into read only mode, and eventually a complete outage.
symptoms: System degradation (read only) followed by a site outage, with the Stack Exchange network going into read-only mode for approximately 5 minutes before being offline for 12 minutes.
root_cause: The primary SQL Server (NY-SQL02) triggered a bugcheck on the SQL Server process, which initially put the SQL server into a read-only state.
resolution: A restart of the SQL service on NY-SQL02 brought the network back online in read-only mode, followed by a sanity check on SQL health before returning to read-write mode.
summary: On January 24, 2017, the Stack Exchange network experienced a critical outage due to a bugcheck on the primary SQL Server, causing a read-only state and subsequent downtime. The issue was resolved by restarting the SQL service and performing health checks.

title: Sweden incident
description: Use of different rulers by builders caused the _Vasa_ to be more heavily built on its port side and the ship's designer, not having built a ship with two gun decks before, overbuilt the upper decks, leading to a design that was top heavy. Twenty minutes into its maiden voyage in 1628, the ship heeled to port and sank.
symptoms: The ship heeled to port and sank twenty minutes into its maiden voyage, resulting in thirty deaths.
root_cause: The ship was asymmetrical due to the use of different rulers based on varying standards of measurement, leading to an unstable design.
resolution: Not specified
summary: The Swedish warship Vasa sank shortly after its maiden voyage due to instability caused by asymmetrical construction from differing measurement standards used by builders. This critical design flaw led to the ship heeling to port and ultimately sinking.

title: Tarsnap incident
description: A batch job which scans for unused blocks in Amazon S3 and marks them to be freed encountered a condition where all retries for freeing certain blocks would fail. The batch job logs its actions to local disk and this log grew without bound. When the filesystem filled, this caused other filesystem writes to fail, and the Tarsnap service stopped. Manually removing the log file restored service.
symptoms: Tarsnap service went offline, monitoring systems alerted the engineer, and users experienced failures in creating and retrieving archives.
root_cause: The log of blocks being freed filled the filesystem, causing other filesystem writes to fail and prompting an immediate shutdown of the Tarsnap service.
resolution: Manually removing the log file restored the Tarsnap service.
summary: The Tarsnap service outage was caused by a filled filesystem due to an unbounded log growth, leading to failures in creating and retrieving archives. The service was restored by manually removing the log file.
