title: Therac-25 incident
description: The Therac-25 was a radiation therapy machine involved in at least six accidents between 1985 and 1987 in which patients were given massive overdoses of radiation. Because of concurrent programming errors, it sometimes gave its patients radiation doses that were thousands of times greater than normal, resulting in death or serious injury.
symptoms: Patients received massive overdoses of radiation, resulting in death or serious injury.
root_cause: Concurrent programming errors in the Therac-25 software.
resolution: Not specified
summary: The Therac-25 radiation therapy machine experienced concurrent programming errors that led to patients receiving overdoses of radiation, causing fatalities and serious injuries. The incident highlights the critical need for robust software testing and error handling in medical devices.

title: Twilio incident
description: In 2013, a temporary network partition in the redis cluster used for billing operations, caused a massive resynchronization from slaves. The overloaded master crashed and when it was restarted, it started up in read-only mode. The auto-recharge component in This resulted in failed transactions from Twilio's auto-recharge service, which unfortunately billed the customers before updating their balance internally. So the auto-recharge system continued to retry the transaction again and again, resulting in multiple charges to customer's credit cards.
symptoms: Erroneous credit card charges, account suspensions, delayed usage reports, and failure of services relying on the redis-master.
root_cause: A temporary network partition in the redis cluster caused a massive resynchronization from slaves, leading to an overloaded master that crashed and restarted in read-only mode.
resolution: The redis-master was restarted to address the high load, and the billing system was taken offline to prevent further erroneous charges. Refunds for erroneous charges were processed.
summary: Twilio's billing system experienced erroneous credit card charges and account suspensions due to a network partition in the redis cluster, which overloaded the master. The issue was resolved by restarting the redis-master and taking the billing system offline to prevent further charges.

resolution: Not specified

root_cause: Network partitions in the cluster caused by maintenance at the datacenter.
resolution: A rolling restart of the cluster was performed, which resolved the network partition.
duration: Approximately 17 hours from the start of the incident until resolution.
summary: Yeller experienced severe processing delays due to network partitions caused by datacenter maintenance, impacting exception processing and user account management. A rolling restart of the cluster resolved the issue.

title: Zerodha incident
description: The Order Management System (OMS) provided to Zerodha, a stock broker, collapsed when an order for 1M units of a penny stock was divided into more than 0.1M individual trades against the typical few hundreds, triggering a collapse of the OMS, which was not encountered prior by its provider - Refinitiv (formerly Thomson Reuters), a subsidiary of the London Stock Exchange.
symptoms: Technical issues with the order management system (OMS) were observed, leading to clients facing additional margin blocks for open orders executed during the affected period.
root_cause: A single order for 1M units of a penny stock was divided into over 100,000 individual trades, causing an overload in the OMS.
resolution: Refinitiv is working on a fix for the OMS to handle such cases, and a temporary limit of 20,000 units per order for equity trades has been implemented.
summary: The OMS experienced technical issues due to an unprecedented division of a large order into over 100,000 trades, leading to client margin block issues. A temporary limit on order quantity has been implemented while a fix is being developed.

title: Zerodha incident
description: A failure of the primary leased line to a CTCL between a stock broker and a stock exchange led to the activation of a backup leased line that was operating sporadically over the following hour, affecting bracket and cover orders. Subsequently, the process of placing and validating orders had been modified to incorporate the unreliability of the CTCL's leased lines, but the reliability of the primary and the backup leased lines was not fundamentally improved by the providers.
symptoms: Hanging orders and positions, orders not getting placed on the exchanges, client complaints regarding bracket and cover orders.
root_cause: Failure of the primary leased line and subsequent unreliability of the backup leased line.
resolution: Shut off the affected CTCL to prevent new orders, lapsed and reconciled pending orders with the exchange, planned a new release to improve exit options for bracket and cover orders.
summary: The incident involved hanging orders due to the failure of a primary leased line and an unreliable backup, affecting a small percentage of clients. The affected CTCL was shut down to prevent further issues, and a new release is planned to enhance order exit options.
